headline: Abu Dhabi’s competition for the global rich takes a new form: divorce law
summary: The emirate has introduced a secular court that handles non-resident family disputes quickly online
body_text:
This August, a British lawyer holidaying in the Arctic Circle made a few quick taps on a laptop and filed what he expected would become the biggest divorce claim in Abu Dhabi’s history.

Byron James had entered the paperwork on behalf of a woman with a Caribbean passport. She was seeking a financial settlement against her husband of two decades, claiming Dh1bn ($270mn), which her lawyer believed was a fair proportion of the man’s wealth.

Thanks to a speedy new secular court, divorcing in Abu Dhabi is now attractive in comparison to popular but overburdened courts such as those in England, says James. In England, complicated cases can take a year or more, cost hundreds of thousands of pounds and expose private affairs to public scrutiny.

For his wealthy clients considering Abu Dhabi, “you’ve got this other system, which hopefully can produce just as good an outcome but does so in a fraction of the time — it’s obvious which one they’re going to choose.”
